claim.content: One-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle calculations that fully include relativistic spin–orbit coupling, performed for two structural models of CsPbBr3, yield the following valence-band hole effective masses and quasiparticle band gaps: for the cubic perovskite structure, an effective hole mass at the R point evaluated along the X–R direction of m_{h}^{*}=0.15\,m_{0} with a quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}=1.74\ \mathrm{eV}; for an orthorhombic structural unit cell, a G_{0}W_{0} calculation (with the valence band Wannier-interpolated for the purpose of band-curvature averaging) yields an averaged hole effective mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} with quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}=2.09\ \mathrm{eV}. These one-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle results include electron–electron correlation effects via the GW self-energy but do not include electron–phonon self-energy contributions (i.e., they are bare-band quasiparticle properties with respect to phonons).

conclusion.content: One-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle calculations that fully include relativistic spin–orbit coupling, performed for two structural models of CsPbBr3, yield the following valence-band hole effective masses and quasiparticle band gaps: for the cubic perovskite structure, an effective hole mass at the R point evaluated along the X–R direction of m_{h}^{*}=0.15\,m_{0} with a quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}=1.74\ \mathrm{eV}; for an orthorhombic structural unit cell, a G_{0}W_{0} calculation (with the valence band Wannier-interpolated for the purpose of band-curvature averaging) yields an averaged hole effective mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} with quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}=2.09\ \mathrm{eV}. These one-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle results include electron–electron correlation effects via the GW self-energy but do not include electron–phonon self-energy contributions (i.e., they are bare-band quasiparticle properties with respect to phonons).

premises.content: In the orthorhombic-structure G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle calculation that produced the averaged hole effective mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0}, the construction of maximally-localized Wannier functions (Wannierization) was applied only to the valence-band manifold for the purpose of interpolating and averaging band curvatures whereas the conduction-band manifold was not Wannierized; this partial (valence-only) Wannier interpolation procedure can introduce a systematic bias in the interpolation of quasiparticle band curvatures and may lead to a modest overestimation of the reported averaged hole effective mass relative to a calculation in which both valence and conduction bands are treated consistently in the Wannier interpolation or in which no post-processing interpolation is used.

Numerical quasiparticle properties computed within the one-shot G_{0}W_{0} approximation, including the valence-band effective mass m_{h}^{*} and the quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}, are sensitive to specific computational choices and convergence parameters such as basis-set completeness, density and sampling of k points in the Brillouin zone, the frequency-integration scheme or plasmon-pole model used for the screened Coulomb interaction, the numerical treatment of spin–orbit coupling, details of pseudopotential versus all-electron implementations, and whether the GW calculation is performed as a single-shot (G_{0}W_{0}) or with partial self-consistency; consequently, different reasonable implementations or convergence thresholds of the G_{0}W_{0} method can produce quantitatively different values of m_{h}^{*} comparable to the differences observed between the cubic and orthorhombic calculations reported here.

steps.reasoning:
State the computational approach used and define notation: the one-shot GW quasiparticle method is denoted $G_{0}W_{0}$ (an instance of many-body perturbation theory in the GW approximation) and spin–orbit coupling is explicitly included; calculations were performed with the all-electron FLEUR and SPEX codes, which implement the $G_{0}W_{0}$ method and include spin–orbit coupling. 
[36],[37],[38]

Explain the purpose of using $G_{0}W_{0}$ for comparison with ARPES: $G_{0}W_{0}$ yields quasiparticle self-energy corrections to electronic states and therefore produces quasiparticle band structures and band gaps that are directly comparable to ARPES spectra; these $G_{0}W_{0}$ calculations include electron–electron correlation but do not include electron–phonon (polaronic) self-energy contributions, so they provide a "bare-band" reference in searches for polaronic mass enhancement.

Report the $G_{0}W_{0}$ result for the cubic crystal structure and define symbols: for the cubic structural model (with lattice parameter 6.017 Å taken from the cited structural data), the $G_{0}W_{0}$ calculation evaluated the valence-band maximum (VBM) at the R point and, along the X–R direction, yields a valence-band hole effective mass $m_{h}^{*}=0.15\ m_{0}$ and a quasiparticle band gap of $1.74\,$eV. The labels $m_{h}^{*}$ and $m_{0}$ are as defined above and the band gap is the energy difference between valence-band maximum and conduction-band minimum in the $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle spectrum. 
Fig. 1

Report the $G_{0}W_{0}$ result for the orthorhombic structural unit cell and note methodological details and caveats: a $G_{0}W_{0}$ calculation performed for an orthorhombic unit cell, with a Wannierization procedure applied for the valence band only (i.e., maximally-localized Wannier functions used for the valence manifold), yields an averaged hole effective mass over crystallographic directions of $m_{h}^{*}=0.226\ m_{0}$ and a quasiparticle band gap of $2.09\,$eV; the authors note that because Wannierization was only performed for the valence band there is a possibility that the effective mass is slightly overestimated in the orthorhombic calculation, but they believe such overestimation is limited. 
Fig. S4

Summarize the relationship of these calculations to electron-phonon coupling: explicitly state that the reported $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle band gaps and effective masses include electron–electron correlation effects but omit electron–phonon (polaronic) self-energy contributions, so any discrepancy between $G_{0}W_{0}$ masses and experiment could be indicative of additional electron–phonon renormalization but the $G_{0}W_{0}$ numbers themselves do not contain such polaronic mass enhancement.

motivating_questions.content: Theoretical predictions of carrier effective masses in lead-halide perovskites have been confounded by sensitivity to the structural phase (cubic vs orthorhombic), by the tendency of common DFT and hybrid-DFT functionals to underestimate band gaps, and by differing levels of many-body treatment in the literature. Since effective mass correlates with the quasiparticle band dispersion and hence with the gap, computations that underestimate the gap or omit important many-body corrections can yield band masses that are not directly comparable to ARPES. A GW calculation that provides a more accurate quasiparticle gap and includes spin–orbit coupling is therefore required to establish a reliable electronic baseline (excluding electron–phonon interactions) against which any additional polaronic mass renormalization could be identified.

paper:867765810263752934.en_title: Is there a polaron signature in angle-resolved photoemission of CsPbBr3?
paper:867765810263752934.zh_title: 在CsPbBr3的角分辨光电子能谱中是否存在极化子特征？
